caveats: a: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for six-digit NAICS 541850.
a: The occupation profile reports headcount shares rather than compensation shares and does not fully expose installation or maintenance occupations.
a: Advertising-industry AI evidence includes brands, agencies, publishers, and digital media rather than only display operators.
rho: General advertising adoption may run ahead of traditional local billboard operators.
rho: Declared use or intent does not establish sustained workflow integration or labor avoidance.
rho: Digital and static display operators may implement at very different rates.
e: Public sources do not measure eligibility in the frozen lower-middle-market population.
e: The industry combines display creation and placement with varying degrees of asset ownership, which affects transferability but not necessarily service eligibility.
s5: Gallup covers all employer industries rather than display advertising specifically.
s5: One large consolidator's acquisition count may not represent the probability for each eligible firm.
s5: Asset purchases do not always constitute qualifying firm control transfers.
q: Clear Channel and Lamar are much larger and more asset-intensive than many lower-middle-market firms.
q: The industry includes creative and operational services whose prices may track labor more closely.
q: Retention could be lower if self-service buying shifts bargaining power to advertisers or platforms.
d5: Industry revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality demand quantity.
d5: The 4.5% forecast was published in a 2024 industry report and ends in 2029 rather than 2031.
d5: Trade-association measures may not capture weaker segments or small-operator dispersion.
o: The estimate weights the asset-operating core more heavily than design-only services within the NAICS code.
o: Software could disintermediate local selling faster than expected even while a physical asset operator remains.
o: Human content approval is an industry best practice rather than a universal legal mandate.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Advertisers and agencies buy or lease access to location-specific billboard, transit, airport, street-furniture, retail, and other display inventory, commonly in campaign periods or network packages. Rates are driven primarily by location, audience, demand, competition, format, and occupancy; operators may also charge for design, printing, installation, and measurement.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house advertising units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo nonemployers, general advertising agencies classified elsewhere, media-buying agencies, sign manufacturers whose primary activity is fabrication, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat public-display advertising services to external customers, including campaign design, inventory sales, billboard and place-based display placement, digital-display management, printing, installation, and related measurement and account service.

a.bridge: A 2026 industry profile reports that management, business and financial, arts/design/media, sales, and office-support occupations together represent 89% of the workforce in Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising. Advertising-industry AI use cases and buyer surveys are used to assign greater exposure to creative generation, prospecting, media planning, inventory matching, proposals, campaign setup, optimization, measurement, and administration, and lower exposure to relationship negotiation, physical installation, inspection, maintenance, and field operations. The source provides employment rather than wage weights and may overstate exposure where senior sales and management wages dominate.
caveats: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for six-digit NAICS 541850.
The occupation profile reports headcount shares rather than compensation shares and does not fully expose installation or maintenance occupations.
Advertising-industry AI evidence includes brands, agencies, publishers, and digital media rather than only display operators.
a.change_evidence: A representative wage-weighted timesheet study of 541850 firms, segmented by sales, creative, campaign operations, administration, installation, maintenance, and management and adjusted for software already in production, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is concentrated in cognitive, creative, commercial, and administrative work that AI can assist or partially substitute. Clear Channel is already piloting AI-enabled sales tools, while industry evidence shows AI use across planning, audience selection, forecasting, creative, and performance analysis. The opportunity is capped by high-value local selling, direct negotiations, brand judgment, and hands-on display operations.

d5.bridge: OAAA reported record U.S. OOH revenue of $9.46 billion in 2025, up 3.6%, with digital OOH up 10.5% and 36.3% of total revenue. Its 2024 facts report cited a 4.5% annual OOH ad-sales forecast for 2025 through 2029. These nominal revenue measures are converted into a lower five-year constant-price, constant-quality service-quantity estimate by discounting for media-rate inflation, digital mix shift, and the older forecast endpoint. This may still overstate real volume if pricing drives reported growth, or understate it if digital screens create materially more sellable campaign slots.
caveats: Industry revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality demand quantity.
The 4.5% forecast was published in a 2024 industry report and ends in 2029 rather than 2031.
Trade-association measures may not capture weaker segments or small-operator dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: A current U.S. five-year OOH forecast separating real impressions, occupancy, number of campaigns, rate inflation, static versus digital inventory, and local versus national spend would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: The medium has recorded sustained recent growth, with digital conversion, programmatic access, better measurement, and a broad local advertiser base expanding use. Demand is cyclical and correlated with GDP, and display operators compete with fast-growing digital channels, so a recession or weak attribution could produce a flat or declining five-year outcome despite the positive base case.

caveats: Public sources do not measure eligibility in the frozen lower-middle-market population.
The industry combines display creation and placement with varying degrees of asset ownership, which affects transferability but not necessarily service eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A stratified audit of the frozen firm universe using Business Register descriptions or commercial profiles, coding external customers, repeat revenue, asset operations, owner dependence, and actual primary activity, would replace this judgment.
e.rationale: NAICS 541850 is defined around creating, designing, or placing public display advertising for customers, so most employer firms in the lower-middle-market are external-service businesses within the lens. Deductions cover misclassified sign fabrication, owner-dependent operations without transferable systems, entities holding isolated sites without an operating service, and businesses without repeat customers or campaigns.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541850 ($2,510,459,678) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541850 ($8,196,645,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $382,092 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541850); class <5: 1,543 firms, avg revenue/firm $457,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $71,557 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 283 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,808,318 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $283,002 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 130 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,468,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $542,742 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,111,917 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,895,515 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,150,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,318,020 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $268,195,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,972,559 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 112

caveats: The estimate weights the asset-operating core more heavily than design-only services within the NAICS code.
Software could disintermediate local selling faster than expected even while a physical asset operator remains.
Human content approval is an industry best practice rather than a universal legal mandate.
o.change_evidence: Operator and advertiser cohort data quantifying self-service programmatic purchases, direct platform buying without local sales support, outsourced versus internal creative, and the share of revenue still requiring human content approval and physical operations would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five service quantity should still require an accountable display operator because someone must control or contract for permitted sites, maintain and inspect physical structures, schedule inventory, meet landlord and municipal obligations, and approve content. Programmatic and agentic tools can automate discovery, planning, buying, creative, and reporting, but they do not eliminate the permitted physical network. Some agency-like planning, creative, and low-touch sales demand can become self-service or shift entirely to software, preventing a near-one estimate.

q.bridge: Clear Channel's 2025 filing shows that most roadside advertising is sold in four-week periods and that rates depend on location, demand, competition, display size, occupancy, reach, and impressions rather than labor hours. This large-operator revenue model is used to infer above-average retention of implemented labor savings for lower-middle-market operators: media-space pricing does not mechanically fall when internal labor hours decline. The proxy may overstate retention for design, printing, installation, or agency-like firms and where programmatic price transparency accelerates competition.
caveats: Clear Channel and Lamar are much larger and more asset-intensive than many lower-middle-market firms.
The industry includes creative and operational services whose prices may track labor more closely.
Retention could be lower if self-service buying shifts bargaining power to advertisers or platforms.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and campaign-level data from a representative 541850 panel showing revenue by inventory rent versus labor-linked services, renewal pricing, discounts, commissions, platform fees, occupancy, and margins before and after automation would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Inventory-based and audience-based pricing gives an operator room to retain savings in sales preparation, creative versioning, scheduling, reporting, and administration. Over five years, advertisers, agencies, platforms, and competitors will still capture part of the gain through negotiation, lower service fees, faster turnaround expectations, and transparent programmatic markets, but the permitted location remains the scarce product.

rho.bridge: IAB's 2025 survey of more than 500 advertising buy- and sell-side experts found only 30% had fully integrated AI across the campaign lifecycle, while half of those not fully integrated expected to do so by 2026. A 2026 buyer survey shows strong intent for AI in analysis, creative optimization, planning, and inventory evaluation but lower intent for direct deal execution and negotiations. These broader advertising populations anchor five-year implementation in display operators; the estimate rises above current full integration as tools mature, then is discounted for smaller-firm resources, legacy inventory systems, data quality, content governance, and relationship-based sales. It may overstate small-operator integration but understate turnkey platform adoption.
caveats: General advertising adoption may run ahead of traditional local billboard operators.
Declared use or intent does not establish sustained workflow integration or labor avoidance.
Digital and static display operators may implement at very different rates.
rho.change_evidence: A 541850 operator survey measuring production deployment, percentage of workflows and campaigns touched, hours saved, system integration, human-review load, and results by firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years is enough for standard tools to reach campaign planning, creative versioning, lead prioritization, yield support, reporting, and back-office work. Implementation should remain incomplete because nearly two-thirds of the 2025 IAB respondents cited data quality, protection, and tool fragmentation as leading barriers, and high-risk content and negotiations retain human control.

s5.bridge: Gallup's U.S. employer-business survey supplies a 22% five-year sell-or-transfer anchor. The base is adjusted upward because Lamar completed more than 50 outdoor-advertising asset acquisitions in 2025 and more than 20 in 2024, including customer contracts and site-location intangibles, demonstrating unusually active consolidator demand. The adjustment may overstate qualifying firm-control transfers because many reported transactions were asset packages rather than changes of control of eligible firms.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer industries rather than display advertising specifically.
One large consolidator's acquisition count may not represent the probability for each eligible firm.
Asset purchases do not always constitute qualifying firm control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based five-year transaction census for U.S. 541850 firms in the frozen size band, separating control sales from isolated billboard purchases, internal reorganizations, closures, and non-control investments, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: Scarce permitted sites, long-lived location rights, customer lists, and local scale create strategic acquisition value and a repeat buyer universe. These features support a transfer probability above the all-employer anchor, but some deals transfer only selected faces or leases, and local operators may remain family-owned or too owner-dependent for a qualifying sale.

principal_driver: Scarce permitted display inventory lets an operator retain AI savings from a workforce concentrated in sales, creative, analytical, and administrative work.
principal_weakness: Asset and lease quality, not labor efficiency, determines much of the business value, while programmatic self-service can erode the local sales moat.
